ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03301710 covers yardlong bean as a raw ingredient; the stir-fried preparation is not separately coded. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers fresh beans with pods as a category; no specific code exists for stir-fried long beans. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 256329006 identifies the yardlong bean substance; the cooked dish is not separately coded. Closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant side serving of stir-fried long beans is approximately 150 g, equivalent to a small bowl or side plate portion.

nutrient_source.title: Yardlong bean, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 11054)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy data for cooked yardlong beans used as the closest approved source. Values adapted to reflect stir-frying with a small amount of oil (~5 g oil per 100 g vegetable), which adds approximately 4–5 kcal and 0.5 g fat per 100 g. Sodium adjusted upward to reflect typical soy sauce seasoning in Chinese stir-fry preparation (~200 mg/100 g). All adaptations are conservative and documented.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care, American Diabetes Association (peer-reviewed journal); data consistent with University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value exists for stir-fried long beans. GI of 20 is estimated from the GI of cooked green beans / leguminous pods (GI ~15–30, mean ~20) as reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 and consistent with University of Sydney GI Database entries for green beans and yardlong beans. GL per serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (20 × 7.5 × 1.5) / 100 = 2.25, rounded to 2.3.

hypertension.guidance: Stir-fried long beans prepared with soy sauce contribute approximately 200 mg sodium per 100 g; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium preparation or limit portion size to manage daily sodium intake below 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and high dietary fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic index of approximately 20 and 3.3 g dietary fibre per 100 g, stir-fried long beans produce a minimal postprandial glucose response and are suitable as a regular vegetable side dish for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, high fibre, and plant-based profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Stir-fried long beans are low in saturated fat (0.3 g/100 g), contain no cholesterol, and provide dietary fibre and folate, making them a heart-healthy vegetable choice; sodium from soy sauce should be moderated in individuals with concurrent hypertension.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-fried long beans (炒长豆角) is a classic Chinese vegetable dish made from yardlong beans (Vigna unguiculata subsp. sesquipedalis), also known as snake beans or asparagus beans, which can grow up to 90 cm in length. In Chinese cuisine, the beans are typically cut into 5–8 cm segments and stir-fried over high heat in a wok with garlic, vegetable oil, and soy sauce, producing a tender-crisp texture with a slightly savory, mildly sweet flavour. The dish is a staple side vegetable in Chinese home cooking and restaurant menus across Singapore, Malaysia, and China. Nutritionally, stir-fried long beans are low in calories (approximately 52 kcal per 100 g), provide moderate protein (2.8 g), dietary fibre (3.3 g), folate (62 mcg DFE), and vitamin C (13 mg), while remaining low in fat and saturated fat. The glycemic index is low (approximately 20), making the dish suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium content is moderate due to soy sauce seasoning (~200 mg/100 g) and should be considered by individuals with hypertension. The dish is naturally vegetarian, vegan, and dairy-free. In Singapore, it is commonly served as a side dish (配菜) alongside rice or noodles. The restaurant item 'Emerald Long Beans' (嫩长豆角) from See Ya Tonight refers to a premium stir-fried preparation of tender young long beans.
common_user_queries: Is 炒长豆角 good for diabetics?
Chinese long beans vs green beans nutrition

confidence_reason: No exact match for stir-fried long beans found in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Nutrient values adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked yardlong bean data with conservative adjustments for stir-frying oil and soy sauce sodium. GI estimated from analogous cooked leguminous pod data in Atkinson et al. 2008. Confidence is medium due to adaptation rather than exact source match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked yardlong beans (NDB 11054), with adjustments for stir-frying oil (~1.5 g added fat per 100 g) and soy sauce sodium (~200 mg/100 g). 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero for plant-based stir-fry with no hydrogenated oils). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero; long beans are a plant food containing no vitamin B12). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero; long beans contain negligible vitamin D). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero; plant-based food contains no dietary cholesterol). 3. GI of 20 is an analogous estimate based on cooked green beans and yardlong beans from Atkinson et al. 2008 and University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for stir-fried long beans was found in approved sources. 4. The restaurant name 'Emerald Long Beans' (嫩长豆角) from See Ya Tonight (field survey data) refers to a premium stir-fried preparation of tender young long beans; the canonical food name has been normalised to 'Stir-Fried Long Beans'. 5. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are estimated from USDA fatty acid profile for vegetable oil used in stir-frying. 6. Hindi local name 'लंबी फलियाँ炒' uses a mixed script (Devanagari + Chinese character 炒 for 'stir-fried') as a pragmatic representation; a fully Devanagari alternative 'लंबी फलियाँ' is also included in aliases. 7. Sentinel value -1 is not used in this record; all fields are populated with numeric values from the adapted source or confirmed zero values as documented above.
